channel.title: FT – Fiction
channel.description: Fiction reviews and articles from the Financial Times.

item.title: The best books of the week
item.description: From David Willetts on liberalism to Gisèle Pelicot on audio — plus saving woodland, an Alan Bennett interview, Howard Jacobson’s angry new novel and a tale of Iran’s brave women

item.title: Howl — Howard Jacobson lambasts antisemitism in his angriest novel yet
item.description: With savage humour, the British Jewish author takes aim at hatred of Jews in the wake of the October 7 atrocity

item.title: Now I Surrender — Álvaro Enrigue’s tale of how the west was really won
item.description: Mixing archival fragments, confessional auto-fiction and picaresque storytelling, an acclaimed account of the Apache struggle is now published in English

item.title: Women Without Men — Shahrnush Parsipur’s powerful tale of misogyny and escape in Iran
item.description: The Booker-longlisted novel follows the interwoven stories of five characters in search of new, sometimes surreal, destinies

item.title: The prescience of Valley of the Dolls
item.description: Sixty years after it was published, the pulp fiction classic is embedded in pop culture and the fashion imagination

item.title: Rebel English Academy by Mohammed Hanif — how to kill an ideology
item.description: The novelist spares no one in a sharp account of a town in Pakistan wracked by political change in the 1970s

item.title: Taiwan Travelogue — a Booker-longlisted novel on the tricks of translation
item.description: At the heart of Yáng Shuāng-zǐ’s rich, heady book is the uneasy bond between a Japanese author and her Taiwanese interpreter

item.title: Look What You Made Me Do — boomers vs millennials in John Lanchester’s London
item.description: The bestselling author of ‘Capital’ returns with a dark allegory of intergenerational conflict

item.title: A Beautiful Loan by Mary Costello — self-discovery steeped in literature
item.description: Seeking clarity on past loves through faith and art, an Irish teacher finds unexpected resolution in Albert Camus

item.title: The best new debut novels — ghosts, down-at-heel aristos and a dead pingpong prodigy
item.description: Tales of a spooky Norfolk village, a shamanic Nigerian astrophysicist — plus the ethics of fiction as murderous revenge

item.title: Banshee — Irish myths get timely makeover
item.description: A new collection of traditional tales reimagined by contemporary writers makes women ‘the fulcrums, not the levers’ of their stories

item.title: Nonesuch by Francis Spufford — angels in the Blitz
item.description: A typist with ambition collides with fascists and an accidental time machine in this intriguing fantasy set in wartime London

item.title: A Better Life by Lionel Shriver — We need to talk (and talk) about immigration
item.description: A wry portrait of intergenerational tensions curdles into a polemic about virtue signalling and liberal border policies

item.title: Favorita — Michelle Steinbeck’s ambitious feminist revenge fantasy
item.description: Hallucinatory episodes and grim reality sit side by side in the story of a daughter out to avenge her mother’s murder

item.title: Repetition: A Novel — Vigdis Hjorth’s story of teenage sexual awakening
item.description: Blurring the boundaries of fiction and memoir, the Norwegian writer revisits the provocative territory of sex and family

item.title: Frieze LA 2026
item.description: LA fires one year later; interviews with Monika Sprüth and Philomene Magers, Greta Waller and Amanda Ross-Ho; artists against ICE. Plus: the problem with celebrity painters

item.title: Brawler by Lauren Groff — fight, flight and fleeting moments of light
item.description: The short stories in this powerful collection excavate the family secrets that shape siblings, spouses and children

item.title: Trip by Amie Barrodale — postmortem parenting
item.description: In a sometimes chaotic novel, a recently deceased woman tries to rescue her marooned autistic son in the land of the living

item.title: America under alien alarm and Egypt after the apocalypse — the best new science fiction
item.description: The latest gripping plot lines include chaos in rural Nebraska, a surreal quest, killer robots and a time-loop murder mystery

item.title: Some Bright Nowhere by Ann Packer — whose death is it anyway?
item.description: An unflinching tale about a woman’s choice of friends over family as the end of her life approaches

item.title: The Disappearing Act by Maria Stepanova — from the belly of the beast
item.description: A celebrated Russian author in exile turns to fiction to navigate the physical and psychological fallout of one’s home country waging war against its neighbour

item.title: Need a guide to a strange city? Pack a novel
item.description: Reading a piece of literature where it was written can transform our relationship with a metropolis, landscape, and the book itself

item.title: Crux by Gabriel Tallent — the thrills and spills of friendship
item.description: A moving and amusing novel explores life’s challenges and choices through two young people’s passion for climbing

item.title: A panoply of intriguing new crime novels that do not disappoint
item.description: From witchcraft to sociopaths and confused kids — plus the latest from Danny Ryan and Nicci French

item.title: The Roof Beneath Their Feet — on higher ground
item.description: International Booker Prize winner Geetanjali Shree’s 2001 novel, now available in English, is a compassionate story of class, desire and friendship
